channel.title: NPR Programs: All Things Considered
channel.description: All Things Considered hosts Ailsa Chang, Mary Louise Kelly, Juana Summers and Scott Detrow present the program's trademark mix of news, interviews, commentaries, reviews, and offbeat features 7 days a week.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: A new phase in the war in Ukraine
item.description: Ukraine says it can now hit military and energy targets deep inside Russia. Former ambassador Daniel Fried explains why he thinks Russia is starting to lose its strategic advantage.
item.content:encoded: Ukraine says it can now hit military and energy targets deep inside Russia. Former ambassador Daniel Fried explains why he thinks Russia is starting to lose its strategic advantage.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Robbins

item.title: Pope Leo visits Lampedusa to spotlight missing migrants
item.description: Pope Leo XIV will spend July 4th in Lampedusa, Italy, one of Europe's busiest migrant landing points. He will pray with migrants and honor those who died trying to cross the Mediterranean to Europe.
item.content:encoded: Pope Leo XIV will spend July 4th in Lampedusa, Italy, one of Europe's busiest migrant landing points. He will pray with migrants and honor those who died trying to cross the Mediterranean to Europe.
item.dc:creator: Megan Williams

item.title: Trump closes out July Fourth with a speech and fireworks
item.description: After a day of extreme heat and canceled events, President Trump closes out America's 250th birthday with a long speech and a fireworks display.
item.content:encoded: After a day of extreme heat and canceled events, President Trump closes out America's 250th birthday with a long speech and a fireworks display.
item.dc:creator: Geoff Brumfiel

item.title: The Supreme Court wraps up a consequential term
item.description: The Supreme Court ended its term with major rulings on immigration, transgender rights and birthright citizenship. LMU Loyola Law School professor Jessica Levinson recaps the biggest cases.
item.content:encoded: The Supreme Court ended its term with major rulings on immigration, transgender rights and birthright citizenship. LMU Loyola Law School professor Jessica Levinson recaps the biggest cases.
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson

item.title: Iran begins week of funeral celebrations for Khamenei
item.description: Foreign dignitaries are gathering in Iran for a week of funeral ceremonies for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, more than four months after he was killed in U.S.-Israeli airstrikes.
item.content:encoded: Foreign dignitaries are gathering in Iran for a week of funeral ceremonies for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, more than four months after he was killed in U.S.-Israeli airstrikes.
item.dc:creator: Hadeel Al-Shalchi

item.title: Reporter's Notebook: The disability rights protest that changed every bus ride
item.description: A 1978 protest by wheelchair users won a right many now take for granted: boarding a city bus. NPR's Joe Shapiro and Colorado Public Radio's Stephanie Wolf explain.
item.content:encoded: A 1978 protest by wheelchair users won a right many now take for granted: boarding a city bus. NPR's Joe Shapiro and Colorado Public Radio's Stephanie Wolf explain.
item.dc:creator: Joseph Shapiro

item.title: From Lead Belly to Tupac: a prison history of music
item.description: In his book 'The Midnight Special', author Colin Asher traces how prisons and the criminal justice system shaped American music, from Lead Belly and Johnny Cash to Tupac.
item.content:encoded: In his book 'The Midnight Special', author Colin Asher traces how prisons and the criminal justice system shaped American music, from Lead Belly and Johnny Cash to Tupac.
item.dc:creator: Daniel Ofman

item.title: How Detroit reflects America
item.description: On Independence Day, NPR's Don Gonyea returns to the city where he started his career to reflect on the auto industry, the Great Migration and the people who built Detroit.
item.content:encoded: On Independence Day, NPR's Don Gonyea returns to the city where he started his career to reflect on the auto industry, the Great Migration and the people who built Detroit.
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson

item.title: Detroit and Windsor, Canada share a summer tradition
item.description: Detroit and Windsor, Canada have shared a fireworks tradition for generations. At the start of summer, thousands of people still gather on both sides of the border to celebrate together.
item.content:encoded: Detroit and Windsor, Canada have shared a fireworks tradition for generations. At the start of summer, thousands of people still gather on both sides of the border to celebrate together.
item.dc:creator: Henry Larson

item.title: The Parade of Presidents has been going since 1976. Meet the woman who keeps it going
item.description: On the main street of Cuba City, Wisc., there's a Parade of Presidents, with decorative shields for each President, begun as a bicentennial project in 1976. Donna Rogers keeps it all going.
item.content:encoded: On the main street of Cuba City, Wisc., there's a Parade of Presidents, with decorative shields for each President, begun as a bicentennial project in 1976. Donna Rogers keeps it all going.
item.dc:creator: Susan Bence
